metaTitle: The Boeing Atmosphere Global Documentation Hub - Components - Select

header.title: Select
header.description: Selects let users select a value from a set of options

items: Selects commonly appear in forms and dialogs
They let the user select a value from a set of options
They show a maximum of 8-9 options

data.resource: Figma: Atmosphere Foundations and Components Library

sections.text: States

Measurements

Select component comes in two density modes: relaxed and condensed.

Usage

A dropdown displays a short list of choices on a temporary surface. They appear when users interact with a button, action, or other control and can be used alone or inside a form.

The user can then select an option that will be reflected in the container.

images.text: Use a select when there are from 3 to 9 select options
Avoid using selects if there are fewer than three options for selection. Use a radio button or a switch instead.
Avoid using  selects if there are more than 9-10 options for selection. Use a search/autocomplete component instead.

sections.textAfter:
1. Container
  2. Label
  3. Trailing Icon
  4. Supporting text (optional)
  5. Placeholder text (optional)
  6. Selected option text
  7. Dropdown
  8. Select Options
  9. Scroll bar (optional)

sections.text:
Container

Contains all the elements of the select. Inherits its styles from the text fields.

Label

Provides information to contextualize the contents of the select. If a select is required, indicate so with an asterisk at the end of the label.

Trailing icon

Icon to visually distinguish that the component will display a dropdown with more information. It will change its direction when the select changes to open state.

Supporting text (optional)

Conveys additional information about the select, such as how it will be used, error messages, etc. It should ideally only take up a single line, though may wrap to multiple lines if required.

Placholder text (optional)

Empty state text that works as a short hint or describes the expected value of an input field. Placeholders can give a preview of the format for the requested information, or instructional text such as “Select an option” to prompt users to interact with the component and make a selection.

Placeholder text is optional and should be used in combination with supporting text. It’s designed to disappear when the user interacts with the component.

Selected option text

Displays the selected option.

Dropdown

Contains all the select options.

Select options

List of all the options that can be selected by the user inside a dropdown. They have their own states.

Scrollbar (optional)

Bar to enable scrolling if all options do not fit in the designated vertical space.
Behavior

Steps

  • When the select is empty, only the label will appear in the center of the field. All of the container will be interactive and the trailing icon will show an arrow.
  • When clicked, the label shrinks to the top left side and a dropdown appears. An optional placeholder can be shown in the container to guide the user.
  • When the user selects one option, the dropdown closes and the input text shows the selected option.
  • Once selected, the selected option can’t be unselected, only changed.

Dropdown direction

By default, dropdowns open downward, but they can open up depending on its position on the screen. If the dropdown appears at the bottom and close to the edge of the interface, the menu expands upward to avoid being cropped.

Scrolling

Scroll bars are optional and depend on the number of options. Starting a scroll at the fourth option in the menu list is recommended, but this may vary based on specific use cases.

Required fields

To indicate that a selection is required, display an asterisk (*) next to the label text and mention in supporting text that the asterisk indicates required fields.

If the user interacts with the select but nothing gets selected, the container will change to an error state. The supporting text should be replaced with error text that describes how to avoid the error.

Cursor

Users can activate the component by clicking on the container element and selecting an option by clicking on an item in the dropdown. The cursor will change to the hand cursor when :pressed.

When the component is :disabled the cursor will not be displayed when hovering or pressing over it and there will be no interaction.

Keyboard

Users should be able to navigate to and activate a select with these keys:

Using labels

Labels must be clear, concise, unique, and accurately describe the selections. Every select component must contain a label. The accessibility label for a select is typically the same as the label for the select.

The values within a select options list should be sorted in a logical order. They can appear in alphabetical order or in increasing order relative to the content. Otherwise, the order should be based on the frequency of use.

Labels, placeholders and supporting texts should be in sentence case.
Responsiveness & densities

sections.textBefore:
Responsive behavior

Selects can show a maximum of 8-9 select options.

Be mindful of responsive layouts where the select can increase or decrease in width. Don’t truncate text unless required as truncated text can impede comprehension. In this case, the label will be truncated with an ellipsis and supported by a tooltip.

sections.text: In mobile devices, selects will follow the default dropdown behavior, but it’s also possible to open the select options inside a radio-button selection dialog depending on use cases.

Densities

The select container and the select options will be reduced in height for condensed versions. Mobile screens should always use relaxed density to ensure touch accessibility. All of these changes can be visually seen in the Measurements section.
